expectations.text: Identifies rl.DrawPixel as the bottleneck because each call goes through the GPU draw pipeline
expectations.evidence: The response states: 'The bottleneck is `rl.DrawPixel` — each call goes through the GPU pipeline individually.'

expectations.text: Recommends the rl.Image (CPU framebuffer) pattern: write to image, upload once, blit
expectations.evidence: The response states: 'The idiomatic raylib pattern for CPU-side per-pixel rendering is the **framebuffer pattern**: write pixels to an `rl.Image` backing buffer, upload once per frame to a texture with `rl.UpdateTexture`, draw the texture once.'

expectations.text: Uses rl.GenImageColor (or similar rl.Image-creating function) for the backing framebuffer
expectations.evidence: `fb_image := rl.GenImageColor(SCREEN_W, SCREEN_H, rl.BLACK)` creates the CPU-side framebuffer.

expectations.text: Uses rl.LoadTextureFromImage to create the GPU texture that mirrors the framebuffer
expectations.evidence: `fb_texture := rl.LoadTextureFromImage(fb_image)` creates the GPU texture.

expectations.text: Uses rl.ImageDrawPixel (or equivalent direct image-data writes) for per-pixel writes, NOT rl.DrawPixel
expectations.evidence: The response uses `cast([^]rl.Color)fb_image.data` to get a direct pointer and writes `pixels[y * SCREEN_W + x] = color` — direct memory writes, not rl.DrawPixel.

expectations.text: Uses rl.UpdateTexture once per frame to upload the framebuffer to the GPU texture
expectations.evidence: `rl.UpdateTexture(fb_texture, fb_image.data)` is called once per frame after the pixel loop.

expectations.text: Uses rl.DrawTexture to blit the texture to the screen each frame
expectations.evidence: `rl.DrawTexture(fb_texture, 0, 0, rl.WHITE)` blits the texture to the screen.

expectations.text: Mentions clearing the framebuffer each frame (rl.ImageClearBackground or equivalent)
expectations.evidence: The response does not call `rl.ImageClearBackground` or any explicit clear. The inner loop overwrites all pixels but no explicit clear call is shown.

expectations.text: Shows a complete main-loop structure from window init through cleanup
expectations.evidence: The response shows the full main loop: `rl.InitWindow`, framebuffer creation, main loop with update/render, and cleanup via defer.

expectations.text: Pairs allocations with defer for cleanup (rl.UnloadImage, rl.UnloadTexture, rl.CloseWindow)
expectations.evidence: The response uses `defer rl.CloseWindow()`, `defer rl.UnloadImage(fb_image)`, and `defer rl.UnloadTexture(fb_texture)`.

expectations.text: Identifies rl.DrawPixel as the bottleneck because each call goes through the GPU draw pipeline
expectations.evidence: The baseline states: '`rl.DrawPixel` issues one draw call per pixel. At 800x600 that's 480,000 draw calls per frame — each one is a full GPU pipeline flush.'

expectations.text: Recommends the rl.Image (CPU framebuffer) pattern: write to image, upload once, blit
expectations.evidence: The baseline states: 'The idiomatic raylib pattern: Render to a CPU buffer, upload as a texture once per frame.'

expectations.text: Uses rl.GenImageColor (or similar rl.Image-creating function) for the backing framebuffer
expectations.evidence: The baseline uses `pixels: [WIDTH * HEIGHT]rl.Color` (a stack array) and constructs `rl.Image{ data = raw_data(&pixels), ... }` directly — NOT `rl.GenImageColor`.

expectations.text: Uses rl.LoadTextureFromImage to create the GPU texture that mirrors the framebuffer
expectations.evidence: `texture := rl.LoadTextureFromImage(image)` creates the GPU texture.

expectations.text: Uses rl.ImageDrawPixel (or equivalent direct image-data writes) for per-pixel writes, NOT rl.DrawPixel
expectations.evidence: The baseline writes `pixels[y * WIDTH + x] = color` directly to the stack array — direct memory writes, not rl.DrawPixel.

expectations.text: Uses rl.UpdateTexture once per frame to upload the framebuffer to the GPU texture
expectations.evidence: `rl.UpdateTexture(texture, raw_data(&pixels))` is called once per frame.

expectations.text: Uses rl.DrawTexture to blit the texture to the screen each frame
expectations.evidence: `rl.DrawTexture(texture, 0, 0, rl.WHITE)` blits the texture to the screen.

expectations.text: Mentions clearing the framebuffer each frame (rl.ImageClearBackground or equivalent)
expectations.evidence: The baseline does not call `rl.ImageClearBackground` or any explicit clear. The inner loop overwrites all pixels but no explicit clear call is shown.

expectations.text: Shows a complete main-loop structure from window init through cleanup
expectations.evidence: The baseline shows the full main loop: `rl.InitWindow`, pixel array and texture creation, main loop with update/render.

expectations.text: Pairs allocations with defer for cleanup (rl.UnloadImage, rl.UnloadTexture, rl.CloseWindow)
expectations.evidence: The baseline uses `defer rl.CloseWindow()` and `defer rl.UnloadTexture(texture)`. Note: no rl.UnloadImage since the image is stack-allocated.

expectations.text: Uses rl.LoadImage to load the PNG into a CPU-side rl.Image
expectations.evidence: `img := rl.LoadImage("assets/texture.png")` loads the PNG into CPU memory.

expectations.text: Uses rl.LoadImageColors (or a documented direct cast of img.data) to get an addressable pixel array
expectations.evidence: `pixels := rl.LoadImageColors(img)` extracts the pixel data as a `[^]rl.Color` array.

expectations.text: Identifies the pixel array type as `[^]rl.Color` — Odin's multi-pointer
expectations.evidence: The response states: 'Type of pixel array: `[^]rl.Color` — a multi-pointer (Odin's equivalent of `Color*` in C).'

expectations.text: Notes that multi-pointers have no length tag and behave like C-style pointers (caller must track bounds)
expectations.evidence: The response states: 'it has no length tag, just the pointer' and 'No bounds checking on multi-pointers — ensure x < width and y < height.'

expectations.text: Notes rl.Color is [4]u8 (or distinct [4]u8) — RGBA 8-bit per channel — and that .r/.g/.b/.a accessors work
expectations.evidence: The response states: '`rl.Color` is [4]u8 (or distinct [4]u8) so `.r/.g/.b/.a` accessors work.'

expectations.text: Shows row-major indexing: pixels[y * width + x]
expectations.evidence: The `sample_pixel` procedure returns `pixels[y * width + x]`.

expectations.text: Notes raylib copies pixel data (or equivalent), so rl.UnloadImage is safe to call immediately after LoadImageColors

expectations.text: Uses rl.UnloadImageColors at shutdown to free the pixel array
expectations.evidence: `defer rl.UnloadImageColors(pixels)` is used at shutdown to free the pixel array.

expectations.text: Uses rl.LoadImage to load the PNG into a CPU-side rl.Image
expectations.evidence: `image := rl.LoadImage(path)` loads the PNG into CPU memory.

expectations.text: Uses rl.LoadImageColors (or a documented direct cast of img.data) to get an addressable pixel array
expectations.evidence: The baseline uses `cast([^]rl.Color)image.data` after `rl.ImageFormat` — a documented direct cast of img.data.

expectations.text: Identifies the pixel array type as `[^]rl.Color` — Odin's multi-pointer
expectations.evidence: The baseline states: 'After `ImageFormat` to `UNCOMPRESSED_R8G8B8A8`, the data is `[^]rl.Color` — a pointer to contiguous `rl.Color` values.'

expectations.text: Notes that multi-pointers have no length tag and behave like C-style pointers (caller must track bounds)

expectations.evidence: The baseline states: 'each is `struct { r, g, b, a: u8 }`' and shows `.r`, `.g`, `.b`, `.a` accessors in the TraceLog call.

expectations.text: Shows row-major indexing: pixels[y * width + x]
expectations.evidence: `sample_pixel` returns `buf.pixels[y * buf.width + x]`.

expectations.text: Notes raylib copies pixel data (or equivalent), so rl.UnloadImage is safe to call immediately after LoadImageColors

expectations.text: Uses rl.UnloadImageColors at shutdown to free the pixel array
expectations.evidence: The baseline uses `rl.UnloadImage(buf.image)` in `unload_pixel_buffer` — NOT `rl.UnloadImageColors`. The pixel pointer is obtained via direct cast, not LoadImageColors, so UnloadImageColors is not used.
